national_holiday.intro: Austria's national day on October 26th commemorates the constitutional law on Austria's ‘perpetual neutrality' adopted in 1955.
national_holiday.history: After the Second World War, Austria was occupied by the Allies for ten years. The State Treaty signed on May 15th, 1955 restored sovereignty. On October 26th, 1955 — one day after the last Allied troops left — the National Council passed the constitutional law on perpetual neutrality. The day has been a national holiday since 1965; before that, the ‘Day of the Flag' on October 26th commemorated regaining sovereignty.
national_holiday.traditions: The Austrian Armed Forces hold a major exhibition on Vienna's Heldenplatz with equipment, demonstrations and interactive stations. Federal ministries and the Federal Chancellery traditionally host an open day. Many families use the public holiday for hikes — ‘fitness marches' are popular tradition.
funFacts: Austrian neutrality is ‘perpetual' and was not imposed from outside — it was self-chosen.
Until 1965, Emperor Franz Joseph's birthday (August 18th) was the unofficial national holiday — the tradition never quite dies.
The ‘army display' on Heldenplatz draws around one million visitors every year.
The October 26th ‘fitness marches' were launched in 1971 by the Interior Ministry to motivate the population to be active.
